access.accessMethod: Request Add MS 17866 using the British Library collection item form; manuscripts are consulted in London.

entities.description: A collateral paired witness of both works, ordered herbal then receipts rather than X 90’s receipts then herbal.
entities.label: British Library Add MS 17866
notes: The current catalog supplies no linked digitized content. This is a dated access finding, not proof that no preservation photography exists.

physicalDescription.extent: The catalog describes one item and contents extending at least through f. 42; the paired collateral versions occupy ff. 5r–21v.
notes: The British Library record broadly describes medicinal virtues, prescriptions, charms, an earlier verse portion beginning at f. 3, and a Latin-English Nomina herbarum at f. 42.
DIMEV and eLALME associate this copy's scribal language with Lincolnshire. That witness-level localization is not a composition place for either abstract work.

bibliographic.citation: Ferdinand Holthausen, “Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift,” Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 18 (Neue Folge 6) (1896): 293–331.
bibliographic.title: Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift

entities.description: Holthausen’s newly collated edition of two distinct verse works in KB X 90. CME00098 begins with “Gereimte Heilkunde” on printed p. 295, continues with “Heilkräuterlehre” on pp. 307–330, and includes the German afterword on p. 331; it omits the article introduction on pp. 293–294.
entities.label: Ferdinand Holthausen, Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift (1896)
notes: The two German poem headings are Holthausen’s supplied labels, not verified manuscript titles.
The first close is dated Gothenburg, November 1895; the afterword is dated Gothenburg, February 1896.

rights.basis: Published in 1896, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff.
rights.component: 1896 edition, transcription, emendations, and editorial matter

rights.basis: Holthausen died in 1956. Swedish Copyright Act section 43 gives the ordinary term through the end of the seventieth year after the author's death, yielding a cautious end date of 2026-12-31 for protectable contributions.
rights.component: Any transcription choices, emendations, notes, or other editorial contribution satisfying the applicable originality threshold
notes: This is a term calculation, not a conclusion that every diplomatic transcription detail is sufficiently original to be protected; re-review on or after 2027-01-01.

bibliographic.citation: George Stephens, 'Extracts in Prose and Verse from an Old English Medical Manuscript, Preserved in the Royal Library at Stockholm,' Archaeologia 30 (1844): 349-418; separately issued, London: J. B. Nichols and Son, 1844.
bibliographic.title: Extracts in Prose and Verse from an Old English Medical Manuscript, Preserved in the Royal Library at Stockholm

entities.description: The first verified printing of the X 90 versions, preserving the manuscript’s raw order: receipt collection, herbal, then displaced receipt continuation.
entities.label: George Stephens, Extracts in Prose and Verse from an Old English Medical Manuscript (1844)
notes: The correct editor is George Stephens (1813–1895), not Thomas Stephens.
Stephens says he copied literally, substituted an easier y for two manuscript letter forms, italicized expanded contractions, and left corruptions uncorrected.

rights.basis: Published in 1844, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff.

entities.description: The direct witness for both poems: receipt collection on manuscript pp. 35–47 with a displaced continuation on pp. 78–80, and herbal on pp. 49–78.
entities.label: Kungliga biblioteket X 90
notes: MEG-C localizes the main hand to Norfolk; this is scribal language, not composition place.
No indexed complete public facsimile was found on 2026-07-13; this is not proof that it has never been digitized.

physicalDescription.extent: 108 leaves / manuscript pages 1–216; target poems on pp. 35–80
physicalDescription.format: Small quarto; approximately 30 lines per page; red rubrics
notes: Stephens gives the main hand as pp. 1–91 and 105–216; MEG-C gives pp. 1–91 and 104–216. Another hand occupies the intervening section.
Stephens reports defective leaves and an old leather binding on cords; the manuscript formerly belonged to Christian Ravius and bore number 28.
physicalDescription.support: Paper except for two parchment leaves

rights.basis: The texts are attested in a fourteenth- or fifteenth-century manuscript and far predate U.S. copyright terms.
rights.component: Underlying anonymous medieval herbal and medical-receipt texts

holding.notes: The IIIF metadata's Call Number value is 'Takamiya MS 46 1,' while its preferred citation and current collection usage identify Takamiya MS 46; both forms are preserved.

entities.label: Beinecke Library Takamiya MS 46

physicalDescription.extent: 43 leaves; the paired verse works occupy ff. 3r-20v
physicalDescription.format: One hand; single columns of 33 lines; English bookhand
notes: The official record dates and locates production in England and describes Middle English and Latin herbal material, red initials, headings, and words, and a modern vellum-board binding.

Yale describes the two opening poems as showing traces of East Anglian dialect; DIMEV/eLALME associates the historical Takamiya copy with southern Lincolnshire. These are witness-level scribal-language claims, not work composition places.

localCopies.label: Complete 114-canvas Yale facsimile of Takamiya MS 46
notes: Complete for all provider-exposed surfaces: covers, pastedowns, front and rear endleaves, ff. 1r-43v, and the laid-in parchment fragment recto and verso. Yale exposes no separately labeled spine or edge views.
The exact IIIF Presentation 3 manifest, 114 full-size images, and per-image URLs, sizes, and SHA-256 values are retained in the bundle inventory.

workPortion.label: Collateral versions of both CME00098 works
notes: The two works share f. 14v / canvas 34. The herbal closes with 'This medicine is proved well a plight'; the red rubric 'For the hede warke' and incipit 'If a man or woman more or lesse' begin the receipt collection on the same surface.
The opening red rubric 'Hic incipiunt virtutes herbarum' and first herbal lines appear on f. 3r / canvas 11; the receipt collection ends on f. 20v / canvas 46.

entities.description: DIMEV v1.0 records the receipt collection and herbal as distinct works, supplies IMEV/NIMEV identifiers, forms and subjects, and enumerates their wider witness traditions.
entities.label: DIMEV records 2343 and 4171
notes: Record 2343 lists six witnesses; record 4171 lists eighteen active witnesses plus historical ghost records.
Record 4171 cites Stephens 1844 pp. 394–395 for X 90, but direct inspection places the herbal on pp. 364–393 and the displaced receipt continuation on pp. 393–395.

rights.basis: Public download verified; no item-level open license was found in the consulted PDF.

lineage.summary: CME00098 encodes Ferdinand Holthausen’s two newly collated X 90 poem editions and German afterword (Anglia 18 (1896), printed pp. 295–331), excluding the article’s title, manuscript introduction, and method on pp. 293–294. The selection contains two distinct anonymous Middle English works: DIMEV 2343, a receipt collection whose displaced continuation Holthausen reinserts into the first poem, and DIMEV 4171, a 965-line herbal. Holthausen directly transcribed Kungliga biblioteket X 90 and collated against George Stephens’s 1844 printing. Complete supporting editions, catalogues, and the collateral Takamiya MS 46 facsimile are cached; no complete public X 90 facsimile was found, and KB’s response about a supplied facsimile and reuse remains pending.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous authors or compilers of two verse works
catalogSummary.date: Medieval composition dates unknown; X 90 main hand dated first quarter of the fifteenth century by MEG-C, with older late-fourteenth-century claims; edited 1895–1896 and published 1896
catalogSummary.region: England; X 90 main hand localized to Norfolk as scribal language; edited in Gothenburg; exact composition places unknown
catalogSummary.title: Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift

catalogingSubject.label: Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is Holthausen’s encoded printed-page selection (Anglia 18 (1896), pp. 295–331): two distinct anonymous Middle English verse works plus his German afterword. CME00098 omits the article title, manuscript introduction, and editorial method on pp. 293–294. It is not one homogeneous medieval work, the complete article or journal, either manuscript witness, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Gereimte Heilkunde / Collection of Medical Receipts in Couplets
notes: The 36-line B-only passage is part of the receipt work’s transmission, not a third abstract work.
contentParts.summary: Sixty-four rubricated Middle English remedy sections in couplets. The encoded 496-line sequence combines X 90’s 460-line main run with a thirty-six-line continuation physically displaced after the herbal.

contentParts.label: Heilkräuterlehre / Of Herbs Twenty-Three
notes: One dotted lacuna row is encoded as L, explaining the 966 raw XML L versus 965 textual-line count.
contentParts.summary: An anonymous Middle English herbal in couplets: a six-line prologue followed by twenty-four numbered plant sections, from betony through fumitory, describing medicinal, ritual, protective, and prognostic properties.

contentParts.label: Nachschrift
contentParts.summary: Two German prose paragraphs dated Gothenburg, February 1896, recording additional readings and corrections to the two editions.

assertion.notes: The interval preserves attributed disagreement; it is not a composition date.

dateStatements.display: Attested in X 90: older KB/Stephens dating 1350–1399; modern MEG-C/LALME dating for the main hand 1400–1425
notes: The works may predate the surviving witness; exact composition dates remain unknown.

dateStatements.display: The X 90 versions were first verifiably printed by George Stephens in 1844

extent.description: Ninety-one represented items across three top-level content parts: a 64-section, 496-L receipt collection; a 25-section (unnumbered prologue plus 24 explicitly numbered plants), 966-L herbal; and a two-paragraph German afterword. The XML contains 89 DIV3, 93 HEAD, 631 MILESTONE, 356 NOTE, and 37 PB. Verse-line count is the raw XML count; Holthausen/DIMEV number the herbal as 965 textual lines because one dotted lacuna occupies an L element.

formStatements.description: Two Middle English verse works plus substantive German prose afterword

scope.description: Rubrics and formulae embedded in the Middle English poems

notes: Holthausen’s singular article title is retained for the modern selection, while DIMEV 2343 and 4171 control the two-work medieval content model.
Norfolk is an X 90 main-hand linguistic localization, and Gothenburg an editorial dateline; neither is asserted as a medieval composition place.
The XML’s 1,462 L elements are a representation count, not a claim that the abstract works have invariant lineation across witnesses.
Historical medical, magical, and prognostic practices described here are not modern medical advice.

next_steps: Review current critical scholarship on DIMEV 2343 and 4171 and the Macer tradition.
Separate authorial or archetypal evidence from witness dates and scribal localizations.
openQuestions.question: When and where were the receipt collection and herbal composed, and what compilation stages produced their divergent witness versions?

scope.description: Scribal language of X 90’s main-hand versions

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous author or compiler of the verse medical receipts
Anonymous author or compiler of the Middle English herbal
notes: The herbal is related to the Macer tradition, but no named English author is supported.

scope.description: Holthausen’s article selection, poem order, and afterword
New collation of X 90 against Stephens 1844

summaries.value: Holthausen’s encoded selection presents two anonymous Middle English medical poems from KB X 90. “Gereimte Heilkunde” is a sequence of sixty-four rubricated remedy sections treating ailments and injuries; Holthausen incorporates a thirty-six-line receipt passage displaced after the herbal in the manuscript. “Heilkräuterlehre” begins with a six-line prologue and treats twenty-four named plants in 965 textual lines, giving medicinal, ritual, protective, and prognostic uses. A German afterword records additional readings and corrections.

CME00098 begins at printed p. 295, not p. 293. It contains 496 encoded verse-line elements for the receipt collection, 966 for the herbal, and two German prose paragraphs on p. 331. The herbal’s raw XML count is one greater than Holthausen’s and DIMEV’s 965-line textual lineation because a dotted lacuna row is encoded as L. The first poem’s 496 lines consist of the 460-line main run plus a 36-line displaced continuation from X 90 pp. 78–80.

notes: Holthausen’s article title, retained for the encoded selection; not an original collective title in X 90.
titles.value: Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift

notes: Discovery translation, not a published alternate title.
titles.value: Medical Poems from a Stockholm Manuscript

notes: Editorial heading meaning “Rhymed Medicine”; not verified in X 90.

titles.value: Collection of Medical Receipts in Couplets
In fowre parties of a man / Be-gynnyth þe sekenesse þat þei han

scope.description: DIMEV’s main-receipt incipit after X 90’s introduction

titles.value: If a man or woman more or less / In his head have great sickness
So þat he non oþer drynke ay, / Ewene ne morwe ne mydday

notes: Editorial heading meaning “Herbal Lore”; not verified in X 90.

titles.supplied_by: Digital Index of Middle English Verse
Digital Index of Middle English Verse

titles.value: Of erbis xxiiij / I wyl þe telle by and by
More of wych Goddys grace / Thynk I to seyn on oþer place

work.author: Anonymous authors or compilers of two verse works
work.dateDisplay: Medieval composition dates unknown; X 90 main hand dated first quarter of the fifteenth century by MEG-C, with older late-fourteenth-century claims; edited 1895–1896 and published 1896
work.displayTitle: Medical Poems from a Stockholm Manuscript
work.preferredTitle: Medicinische Gedichte aus einer Stockholmer Handschrift
work.regionDisplay: England; X 90 main hand localized to Norfolk as scribal language; edited in Gothenburg; exact composition places unknown

A German afterword records additional readings and corrections.
